title: const и readonly: литерал в IL против чтения поля в рантайме

cards.prompt: A const is a compile-time literal baked into the caller's IL. M() => Max compiles to ldc.i4.s (0x1F=31) with the literal 100, then ret (0x2A=42). Expression-bodied gives clean IL with no nop, so GetILAsByteArray prints 31 100 42
cards.code: using System; using System.Reflection; class X { const int Max = 100; public static int M() => Max; } var il = typeof(X).GetMethod("M").GetMethodBody().GetILAsByteArray(); Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", il));

cards.prompt: A readonly field is read at run time, so the method's first IL opcode is ldsfld (load static field), whose byte is 0x7E=126 — not a ldc literal (31). So il[0] prints 126
cards.code: using System; using System.Reflection; class Y { static readonly int Max = 100; public static int M() => Max; } var il = typeof(Y).GetMethod("M").GetMethodBody().GetILAsByteArray(); Console.WriteLine(il[0]);

cards.prompt: A const literal is baked in even when it is a full int. Max=1000 exceeds the short ldc.i4.s range, so it uses ldc.i4 (0x20=32) with the 4-byte little-endian literal 1000 (232 + 3*256), then ret (42), printing 32 232 3 0 0 42
cards.code: using System; using System.Reflection; class Z { const int Max = 1000; public static int M() => Max; } var il = typeof(Z).GetMethod("M").GetMethodBody().GetILAsByteArray(); Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", il));
